The improvement from his first season at Houston to 2025 was noticable and he found himself around the football more; the ball production backs that up. A better athlete than you'd think, the former 3-star recruit and transfer from Oklahoma and USC more than trippled his pass breakups from 2024 with ten as a senior while racking up 51 tackles (38 solo), three tackles for loss, one sack, and one forced and one recovered fumble.

— McCutchin physically attacks the catchpoint and mirrors underneath routes in man coverage with impressive athleticism.
Strengths:
— McCutchin physically attacks the catchpoint and mirrors underneath routes in man coverage with impressive athleticism.

— Best from a press alignment, he can use his length and jam to re-route receivers and throw off play timing.

— Has a quick downhill trigger and flashes the ability to stack and shed in the run game.
Weaknesses:
— His route recognition and zone instincts are still developing, and put him behind route breaks and throws in off coverage.

— The 6'2", 191-pound corner lacks recovery speed when stacked vertically and resorts to grabbing and pulling.

— Struggles locating the ball with his back turned and the ball production shows it.

— A willing run game participant, but he's a poor tackler who lunges and dives at ball carriers.

Name: Bishop Fitzgerald
Summary:
With ten interceptions over the last three seasons, the football just finds Bishop Fitzgerald. He projects best as a split-field safety with a compact NFL frame and good length to wrap up and tackle.

The No. 10 ranked JUCO Prospect transferred to NC State for the 2023 and 2024 seasons, then entered the transfer portal as a 3-star and ended up at USC. He finished 2025 with 51 tackles (34 solo), two and a half tackles for loss, one sack, five interceptions, and three passes defended.

— Fitzgerald is a ballhawking safety who excels reading route concepts and taking the ball away from split-field alignments.
Strengths:
— Fitzgerald is a ballhawking safety who excels reading route concepts and taking the ball away from split-field alignments.

— His zone instincts and feel show up and he stays assignment sound while passing off receivers.

— Quick to trigger underneath, his length and driving power help him bring down ball carriers.
Weaknesses:
— Lacks the recovery speed and lateral agility to function as a post safety and struggles to change direction from his backpedal.

— Eyes wander in man coverage, often losing his responsibility and falling behind the play.

— Fitzgerald overcommits in the run game, opening up backside lanes and giving up big plays as a result.

—While he's a willing tackler, he takes poor angles to the ball carrier and misses tackles.

Name: Landon Robinson
Summary:
Standing at 5'11" and 291 pounds, Landon Robinson may be undersized , but he's a very impressive athlete as a disruptive defensive lineman and intriguing late-round option.

Coming out of high school in the 2021 class, Robinson wasn't ranked on 247Sports' composite rankings and primarily held FCS offers before spending one year at the Naval Academy's Prep School.

He didn't get any playing time for the Midshipmen until 2023, then ended up collecting 153 tackles, 18.5 tackles for loss, 14.5 sacks and two forced fumbles in 38 career games. Robinson earned first-team All-American Conference honors in 2024 and '25, while also earning the league's Defensive Player of the Year this past season.
Strengths:
- Robinson is an underappreciated athlete who tested well during his pro day to earn a 9.46 relative athletic score among defensive tackles.

- Quick get-off to get penetration when one-gapping or slanting.

- Against the run, the fire hydrant-like defensive tackle brings natural leverage with some pop in his hands to stand up interior offensive linemen occasionally. He also takes on blocks with a wide base to hold his gap when left one-on-one.

- Good at sinking his hips to avoid getting washed when slanting.

- As a pass-rusher, he's quick and has active hands to beat offensive linemen across their faces.

- Agile with impressive lateral movement skills to be an effective looper on line games. Also, he is good at finding and exploiting open pass-rush lanes.

- High-motor, high-effort player to get coverage sacks or factor into gang tackles down the field.
Weaknesses:
- Robinson is undersized for an NFL defensive tackle and significantly lacks the desired length.

- Lacks the mass and strength to hold his ground versus double-teams in the ground game or use an effective bull rush against NFL-caliber blockers.

- Short arms lead to limited extension on blocks as a run defender and offensive linemen tend to make first significant contact when rushing the passer, allowing blockers to get into his body.

- Block recognition against the run is a little slow, leading to getting reached or scooped.

- Blended in rather than standing out against the two high-level programs Navy played this past season in Notre Dame and Cincinnati.

Name: Aaron Anderson
Summary:
Aaron Anderson is a precise route-runner with a chain-moving skill set for NFL offenses.

He puts pressure on opposing cornerbacks with his patience, tempo and football IQ, allowing him to run crisp routes.

Anderson's college career began at Alabama. The 4-star recruit in the 2022 class then transferred to LSU in 2023 and spent three seasons with the Tigers.
Strengths:
- Crafty route-runner who defeats man coverage with his quickness. Runs detailed routes with little wasted motion and sharp plants at the apex of his route stem.

- Deploys varying releases to enter his route against different alignments. Able to counter press coverage with good use of hands and intentional footwork.

- Excels at attacking zone coverages. Finds open grass and settles into the voids.

- Does not provide indicators of when he'll snap or sink at the break point.

- Elusive in the open field and in a phone booth to help generate yards after the catch. Effective on short and intermediate routes, along with manufactured touches like screens.
Weaknesses:
- Will not meet the required size measurements for all 32 teams.

- Limited wingspan and catch radius. Will not win contested or high-point passes.

- Struggles to detach from physical, handsy press coverage cornerbacks.

Name: Logan Fano
Summary:
Logan Fano has good genes with four uncles (Gabe and Spencer Reid, Teni and Anton Palepoi) who played in the NFL, and a brother, Spencer, who is also in the 2026 draft class as a projected first-round pick.

Logan is a draftable prospect in his own right due to his combination of size and strength to be a solid run defender and power rusher on the edge.

The 6'5", 260-pounder began his college career as a 4-star recruit in the 2021 class at BYU. He never played for the Cougars because he served a Mormon mission and then transferred to Utah ahead of the 2023 season.
Strengths:
- Has solid size and frame for an NFL edge defender.

- Decent get-off. Reacts to the snap well and has solid acceleration off the line of scrimmage.

- Quick to get his hands up against the run and takes on blocks with good pad level and a wide base. Also has solid upper-body strength to get extension on blocks and help overcome the lack of arm length as a run defender.

- Has no problems defeating blocks against tight ends and can set the edge against average offensive tackles.

- Strength allows him to keep offensive tackles off his body as a pass-rusher. Decent at collapsing the pocket against offensive tackles when bull rushing.

- Has a solid long-arm move that he can win with. Has shown a good countermove off it by engaging with the long arm to get tackles to stop their feet, then using his quickness to win around the edge.
Weaknesses:
- Older prospect who will turn 24 before the end of his rookie season, limiting his ceiling for some teams.

- Very short arms for an NFL edge defender.

- Lacks the athleticism, movement skills and use of hands to be a threat to win with finesse moves as a pass-rusher in the NFL.

- Likes to work a spin move but doesn't knock the offensive lineman's hands down first or gain ground laterally. Ends up just twirling in place instead of winning around the edge.

- Poor bend, which impacts his ability corner at the top of the rush, cover bootlegs effectively or work flat down the line of scrimmage when unblocked against the run.

- Often gets reached or cut off on the back side of zone runs due to subpar block recognition and agility.

- Struggles to sink his hips and anchor when trying to set the edge against good offensive tackles or when down blocking.

- Isn't strong enough to consistently stack and shed versus NFL offensive linemen.

Name: Joshua Braun
Summary:
Joshua Braun is a thick, wide-bodied guard with very good length, adequate play strength, and a consistently urgent play style. He shows good feel for threats, spacing, and timing — routinely securing first-level defenders before releasing on climbs and finding work when uncovered. Braun also brings steady effort to finish, run to the ball, and assist ball-carriers. However, below-average quickness, an upright posture, and a tendency to absorb contact into his frame cap his ability to generate displacement and sustain. He projects as a reliable interior swing backup with spot-start potential.

Braun is a 6'6", 325-pound offensive tackle prospect who entered the Florida program as a 4-star recruit in the 2020 class. He spent three years in Gainsville before transferring to Arkansas for the 2023 and 2024 seasons. Braun ended his career transferring a second time to Kentucky for the 2025 season.

The former Wildcat started 43 career games (seven at Florida, 24 at Arkansas and 12 at UK) at both guard spots (24 RG & 19 LG) before participating in the 2026 Shrine Bowl.
Strengths:
- Thick, wide-bodied frame with very good length and big hands.

- Adequate play strength with a stiff punch in pass protection.

- Good feel on combination blocks to secure adjacent gaps before climbing.

- Alert, urgent play style shows up when uncovered looking for work and consistently running to the ball to finish.

- Extensive experience at both guard spots boosts utility at the next level.
Weaknesses:
- Tall frame and upright play style with middling athletic ability result in shaky control and sustain skills.

- Will play short, negate length and allow defenders into his chest.

Name: Eric Gentry
Summary:
Eric Gentry made waves in the NFL draft community for his impressive frame and excellent length. He's also a good athlete to pique the league's interest. But it's fair to question if that's enough to deserve even being drafted because the tape is rather underwhelming.

The 6'7", 221-pound linebacker was a 3-star recruit in the 2021 high school class and originally attended Arizona State. He spent one season with the Sun Devils, playing in 12 games (four starts) and racking up 45 tackles, five tackles for loss, a sack and two passes defended.

Gentry transferred to USC the following year, where he spent the last four seasons. He participated in 40 games with 26 starts, collecting 226 tackles, 21.5 TFLs, eight sacks, two interceptions, 11 PDs and nine FF for the Trojans.
Strengths:
- Gentry has a great frame with very long arms and plenty of room to add weight.

- Good overall athlete, who moves well to develop as a coverage defender down the line.

- Long strides increase his sideline-to-sideline range, with the speed to carry the seam in Tampa 2.

- Uses a decent shoulder-dip move to make offensive linemen miss occasionally on the second level.

- Arm length helps increase his tackle radius.

- Good at punching the ball out on tackle attempts. He led the Big Ten with five forced fumbles in 2025.

- USC used him in a variety of ways, from an off-ball linebacker to covering the slot and working off the edge. As such, Gentry brings some position and scheme versatility.
Weaknesses:
- Gentry is far too lean, especially for his stature, to be on the field during early downs in the NFL. He's built more like a basketball player, with the need to add build and strength.

- Instincts are lacking in both phases of the game. He often covers grass and is slow to key and diagnose run plays.

- Doesn't use his hands to take on blocks, preventing him from taking advantage of his long arms and getting extension.

- Has a habit of lunging and leaving his feet to make tackles, leading to misses.

- Lacks physicality in general.

- Potential injury concerns, with a concussion history and missed three games in 2022 due to ankle surgery.

Name: Jayden Williams
Summary:
Jayden Williams is a high-cut, wiry tackle with good initial quickness and a jolting punch, generating force on contact with a sturdy anchor and movement skills on cutoffs and pulls. However, his upright, robotic style and wandering eyes lead to late reactions, quick losses and limited recovery, with lapses in timing exposing him to movement. Williams projects as a capable backup who needs to land first and latch, with limited ability to recover once the rep extends.

Williams is a 6'4", 307-pound offensive tackle prospect who entered the Ole Miss program as a 4-star recruit in the 2021 class.

The former Rebel started 34 career games split between each tackle spot (19 at LT & 15 at RT), including 15 at right tackle last season. Williams was a full-time starter as a redshirt freshman in 2022 before finishing 2023 as a role player and missed seven games in 2024 due to knee injuries.
Strengths:
- Wiry play strength with a stiff punch and strong clench once latched.

- Good initial quicks seen on backside cut-offs and leading around the edge on pulls.

- Sturdy anchor to match power with power and dissipate the bull-rush.

- Capable of widening defenders off his edges, redirecting and dying slowly in recovery mode when his initial punch lands.
Weaknesses:
- High-cut build with an upright playing style.

- Robotic blocker with below average redirect and recovery skills.

- Has lapses in snap timing and focus that result in an occasional mental error or being tardy to react to immediate post-snap movement.

Name: Caullin Lacy
Summary:
Caullin Lacy is a quick, sudden in-space playmaker and return specialist with end-of-the-roster value.

His career began at South Alabama, where he enrolled as a former 3-star recruit in the 2020 class. He had his most productive season (2023), amassing 1,316 receiving yards, seven touchdowns, and earning First-team All-Sun Belt honors. In 2025, Lacy earned Third-team All-ACC All-Purpose honors.

Lacy is an experienced football player who has appeared in 64 total games. His path to an NFL 53-man roster is carving out a role as a special teams ace.
Strengths:
- Sudden change of direction with fluid hips and quick feet

- Runs routes with tempo and patience; sets up defenders well

- Excels on shorter/quick passing concepts, creating easy catch and run opportunities

- Can be an impact kick and punt returner; four total return touchdowns in career

- Playmaker in space on manufactured touches; screens, jet pop passes, etc
Weaknesses:
- Slot-dependent receiver; not suited for boundary alignments

- Above average athlete; limited vertical explosiveness and speed

- Small frame and wingspan limit catch radius; Is not a strong contested catch threat

- Older prospect and sixth-year senior

Name: Noah Whittington
Summary:
Noah Whittington is a low-center-of-gravity, well-rounded running back prospect who offers value in both the running and passing games.

Whittington has a natural feel for the running back position and projects as an adequate backup or RB2 in the NFL.

Whittington's career began at Western Kentucky as a former 3-star recruit in the 2020 recruiting class. He has proven to be a steady, consistent player when given touches to produce. He offers special teams value as a kick returner.
Strengths:
- Whittington runs hard with a low center of gravity. He lowers his shoulder and charges downhill, driven by momentum, his strong lower half carrying him through arm tackles.

- Oregon's RB plays with good vision and quick processing. He reads leverage well when pressing the line of scrimmage. Whittington's abilities work well in both gap and zone-schemed concepts.

- He is a patient runner who sticks behind his lead blockers. His shorter stature is used to his advantage, allowing him to hide behind his OL before making his move into the open field. His eyes and feet are paired together as he navigates the line of scrimmage.

- Whittington's lateral agility is impressive. He strings together jukes and patterns to force missed tackles against alley defenders and get out of the backfield.

- The Ducks' versatile RB is a former high school wide receiver, and it shows up when he is asked to run routes out of the backfield. His quick footwork helps freeze second-level defenders on option routes and in/out-breaking routes. He has displayed great hands, plucking the ball away from his frame.
Weaknesses:
- Whittington is not a big-bodied runner and will not check the boxes for some NFL teams. He will need to be paired with a bigger back for those short-yardage situations against NFL defenses.

- Despite a few long runs this season, he is not a breakaway runner with outstanding top-end speed. His acceleration is above average at best; he wins with nuance and not elite athleticism.

- Love his effort in pass protection, but his technique needs to be refined. He will lunge and bend at the waist over his toes, giving up leverage at the POA. Cleaning this up will solidify him as a reliable third-down option in pass pro.

Name: Quintayvious Hutchins
Summary:
Quintayvious Hutchins is a a one-trick pony, but it's a pretty good trick. He's quick with great change-of-direction skills to beat offensive tackles across their faces and make them look silly, providing upside to contribute as an NFL pass-rusher.

The Boston College product was a 3-star recruit in the 2021 high school class and didn't see much defensive action until his redshirt junior season, primarily contributing on special teams and playing tight end in 2022.

He participated in 43 career games (16 starts) and logged 72 tackles, nine tackles for loss and 5.5 sacks for the Eagles.
Strengths:
- Hutchins shows solid burst off the line of scrimmage to threaten offensive tackles with speed around the edge, to go along with a decent cross-chop move.

- Sets up his pass-rush moves by stemming outside to win inside or vice versa, and has very good change of direction beat tackles across their faces.

- Can be effective as the looper in line games with his change of direction and agility.

- Looks comfortable spot dropping in coverage as a change of pace.

- Hustle player, will factor into gang tackles down the field.
Weaknesses:
- Hutchins is too light to set the edge consistently in the NFL, and he didn't test well at the NFL combine to suggest he's a good enough athlete to overcome a lack of size.

- He also has subpar arm length, consistently allowing offensive linemen to make the first significant contact when rushing the passer.

- Against offensive tackles who are patient to avoid oversetting against his stems or can stay in front of him, he lacks the hand usage to get a clean win and will get ridden inside or beyond the quarterback. He's overly reliant on making tackles miss.

- Shows some ankle stiffness that makes cornering at the top of the rush difficult.

- Minimal production as a pass-rusher in college, including recording just two sacks this past season.

Name: Jager Burton
Summary:
Jager Burton is a quick-twitch center prospect who excels reaching landmarks on combo blocks, reaches, pulls and climbs. However, limited play strength and sturdiness create inconsistent contact balance, often compromising his posture and leaving him vulnerable to being pried open when isolated. His positional versatility and movement skills project best to a swing interior backup role in the NFL.

Burton is a 6'4", 312-pound center prospect who entered the Kentucky program as a 4-star recruit in the 2021 class.

The former Wildcat started 47 career games across the interior; 23 at LG, 15 at C and 9 at RG). Burton participated in the 2026 Shrine Bowl.
Strengths:
- Solid quickness and burst to effectively pull, climb and intersect targets on the move.

- Does a solid job of reaching landmarks and getting into initial fits on zone combinations and reach blocks.

- Functional execution on back blocks and as the drive man on doubles using low pad level and burst to line up and cover up his target.

- Proven versatility across the interior.
Weaknesses:
- Marginal contact balance, sturdiness and play strength.

- Posture breaks down when down linemen get into his frame resulting in soft edges and getting pried open.

- Mediocre square power and girth limits effectiveness in the post on doubles and generating movement on vertical run concepts.

Name: Deven Eastern
Summary:
Deven Eastern has good size and strength to make an impact as a run defender at the next level. That should put the 6'5", 315-pound defensive lineman on teams' radars in the late rounds of the 2026 NFL draft.

A 4-star recruit in the 2021 high school class, Eastern was a three-year starter at Minnesota. He racked up 97 tackles, 12 tackles for loss, 4.5 sacks, five passes defended and two forced fumbles in 45 career games, earning honorable mention All-Big Ten honors in 2025.

Matt Holder has been a part of B/R's Scouting Department for four seasons. He also writes about the Las Vegas Raiders and NFL draft for SB Nation's Silver and Black Pride, and co-hosts a YouTube Channel, Tape Don't Lie, providing film breakdowns and more draft prospect analysis.
Strengths:
- Eastern has good size and length for an NFL defensive tackle.

- When he uses his hands to take on blocks, he has plenty of upper-body strength and pop in his hands to lock out interior offensive linemen. Also has long arms to maximize his extension on blocks.

- Against down blocks, he's solid at absorbing contact to avoid getting washed inside.

- Showed a decent arm over move that he can use to escape blocks as a run defender and win with occasionally as a pass-rusher.

- Has the strength to make tackles with offensive linemen hanging onto him.
Weaknesses:
- Eastern has a false step out of his stance as a slow get-off, making it difficult to push the pocket when bull rushing.

- Subpar athleticism overall and use of hands as a pass-rusher, putting him into the two-down player category.

- Plays with high pad level and a narrow base, causing him issues anchoring against combo blocks and double teams.

- Block recognition is often late, resulting in getting reached or trapped.

- Hands are frequently late and wide when taking on blocks in the run game, exposing his chest.

Name: Jaren Kanak
Summary:
Jaren Kanak showcases a natural feel for catching the football as a first-time tight end. He's played quarterback, linebacker, and now tight end from high school through college and is determined to make an NFL roster.

A former 4-star recruit, he made the switch to tight end for his senior season at Oklahoma and quickly earned the trust of quarterback John Mateer, finishing third on the team with 533 yards on 44 receptions.

— Kanak is a natural, reactive catcher of the football with great concentration and hand usage.
Strengths:
— Kanak is a natural, reactive catcher of the football with great concentration and hand usage.

— He's a good athlete and has a knack for exploiting space and grinding out tough yards after the catch.

— Brings alignment versatility to the offensive room with experience as a special teams contributor.
Weaknesses:
— Kanak has a tweener body type with shorter arms that limit his catch radius and impact as a blocker.

— Still learning how to block, the former Sooner has a long way to go with learning technique and hand placement to compete as a blocker.

— He's a limited route runner who wastes movement and needs to learn the nuances of creating separation.

—Struggles against physical, man coverage, and doesn't have the tools to get free from it.

Name: Jack Kelly
Summary:
Jack Kelly has decent instincts and downhill speed as a run defender and can contribute as a pass-rusher to potentially earn an NFL roster spot and stick.

As an unranked player in the 2021 high school class, Kelly's college career began at FCS level for Weber State. He redshirted as a true freshman and saw the field for two seasons with the Wildcats, racking up 85 tackles, 20 tackles for loss, 16.5 sacks, an interception, seven passes defended and five forced fumbles in 28 career games.

The 2023 first-team All-Big Sky performer transferred to BYU for his redshirt junior campaign and immediately became one of the Cougars' starting linebackers. Over 25 games in Provo, he logged 106 tackles, 23.5 TFLs, 15 sacks, an interception, two PDs and four FFs.
Strengths:
- Kelly has a thick build and good weight for an NFL linebacker.

- Solid instincts against the run to be decently quick at key and diagnosing plays, and the linear speed to shoot gaps and make unblocked tackles near the line of scrimmage.

- Solid awareness in zone coverage, showing decent eye discipline to locate threats, and does a good job of working for depth to help defensive backs takeaway crossing routes over the middle.

- Uses his hands well to force reroutes and disrupt the timing of routes against tight ends and wide receivers.

- Offers value as an off-ball blitzer, showing the ability to beat running backs with power and solid closing speed to force the ball out of the quarterback's hand when unblocked or as the looper in line games. Also has good pass-rush production with 35 total sacks in college, including two double-digit sack seasons.
Weaknesses:
- While Kelly tested well at the NFL combine, he looks stiff and his lateral movement skills didn't stand out on tape.

- A lack of quickness and agility makes it difficult to read the quarterback's eyes, tighten throwing windows and/or make plays on the ball in zone coverage.

- Will get beaten by running backs in man coverage, in all areas of the field.

- Stiff knees make it difficult for him to sink his hips and anchor as a run defender against offensive linemen.

- Short arms lead to him getting stuck on blocks. He doesn't get much extension, allowing offensive linemen to hang onto him to make stacking and shedding difficult.

Name: Chase Roberts
Summary:
Chase Roberts is a compact, wide receiver prospect with crisp short-area route running.

Joined BYU as a former 3-star recruit in the 2019 class. He is battle-tested and suffered a hip injury as a freshman, which required surgery. Roberts comes from an athletic family; his father played DB, and his younger brother plays WR at BYU.

Roberts is a two-time team captain and leader for the Cougars. He projects as a practice squad receiver who will need to carve out a role to make the 53-man roster.
Strengths:
- Good stem usage to influence the corner's hips before the breaking point

- Sudden and strong hands to pluck the ball away from his frame and through contact

- Best utilized as an in-breaking route runner; toughness to work in the MoF
Weaknesses:
- Adequate top-end speed; unable to pull away from defenders on the vertical plane

- Lacks the foot quickness to create space at LOS versus bump and run coverage

- Perimeter blocking is below-average; misses his target too often

- Limited elusiveness or wiggle to create after the catch

Name: Joey Aguilar
Summary:
Joey Aguilar is an experienced quarterback prospect with above-average physical tools. He projects as a backup quarterback who will compete for a roster spot.

Aguilar started his college football career at Diablo Valley College. After two years, he transferred to Appalachian State as a former 3-star recruit in the 2023 class. Spent two more seasons developing and earning playing experience before transferring to Tennessee for his final season of college football.

Aguilar proved he could hang with the big boys of the SEC with his performance versus the Georgia Bulldogs in a thrilling game.
Strengths:
- Point-guard play style was effective; distributed the ball to multiple weapons

- Countered edge pressure by climbing vertically in the pocket

- Patience in the pocket; trusted his protection and allowed routes to develop

- Played adequately in one season against top-caliber competition
Weaknesses:
- Anticipating the throwing lane is adequate and inconsistent

- Throws "moon" balls when throwing deep downfield, leaving too much air under passes

- Inconsistent accuracy and ball placement on underneath throws

- Lacks lateral agility or quickness to evade and escape defensive pressure

Name: Alan Herron
Summary:
Alan Herron has well-distributed thickness throughout his frame and delivers solid power on contact, particularly on down blocks and as a drive-man on double-teams. However, tardy, disjointed footwork and late hand timing sap his ability to sustain and consistently protect the corner.

In the NFL, Herron projects him as a career backup whose value is rooted in interior power and situational usage rather than consistent edge protection.

Herron is a 6'5", 308-pound offensive line prospect who enrolled at Division II Shorter University as an unranked recruit in the 2022 class before transferring to Maryland in 2024. He started 24 games at right tackle during his two years at Maryland.
Strengths:
— Solid initial quicks and power to displace, cover up and wall off defenders on down blocks, angle-drives and as the drive man on double-teams.

— Active, firm inside hand in pass protection helps minimize clean losses across his face.

— Effective snatch-trap technique to win leverage against leaning power rushers.

— Showed flashes of being able to widen the corner against high-side moves during Senior Bowl practice sessions.
Weaknesses:
— Struggles to expand his set points and consistently protect the corner against high-side rushes.

— Susceptible to long-arm sequences, with persistent chest exposure leading to an unreliable anchor.

— Inconsistent footwork during the drive phase of run blocks leads to rolling onto his toes, sapping his power and control late in the rep.

Name: DeShon Singleton
Summary:
DeShon Singleton is a long-limbed, heavy-tackling, box safety who set the tone for Nebraska's defense every game. He's going to compete on special teams and bring the wood as an enforcer.

The former 3-star JUCO transfer played four seasons for the Cornhuskers and compiled 163 tackles (98 solo), seven and a half tackles for loss, a sack, three interceptions, and eight passes defensed.

— Singleton plays with an aggressive mindset and wants to make impactful tackles and hits on the ball carrier.
Strengths:
— Singleton plays with an aggressive mindset and wants to make impactful tackles and hits on the ball carrier.

— Showcases physical tight end coverage ability as a big slot and will play the run as a nickel.

— Projects best as a pure box player and closer to the line of scrimmage, where moving downhill with bad intentions in a phone booth is rewarded.
Weaknesses:
— Singleton isn't a natural coverage player, and his lack of change-of-direction skills inhibits his ability against receivers.

— Not an instinctual player when reading routes, and his ball production shows it.

— His eye discipline leaves him behind plays in coverage, and he doesn't have the recovery speed to make up for it.

Name: Aaron Graves
Summary:
At 6'5", 295 pounds, Aaron Graves combines size and solid athleticism to be an intriguing late-round 2026 NFL draft prospect or as a priority free-agent signing. His quickness and movement skills give him potential as a pass-rusher, and he has the upper-body strength to get extension on blocks when working against the run.

Graves went to Iowa as a 4-star recruit in the 2022 class, getting playing time as a true freshman and becoming a full-time starter during his junior year. He played in 52 games for the Hawkeyes, racking up 122 tackles, 25 tackles for loss, 16.5 sacks, four forced fumbles, a sack and five passes defended over four seasons.

That production resulted in honorable mention (2024) and second-team (2025) All-Big selections for the Iowa native.
Strengths:
- Graves has good size and appears to have long arms. Fits the physical profile for an NFL 4i-technique.

- Decent overall athlete who shows solid quickness, giving him potential to develop as a pass-rusher.

- Sets up his pass-rush moves well by stemming outside to open up an inside lane or vice versa.

- Has flashed a solid swim-move that he can win with as a pass-rusher or use to escape blocks in the run game.

- When he picks his hand straight up from the ground, he has the upper-body strength and length to get extension blocks in the run game.

- Solid at moving his feet and using his agility to avoid getting reached.
Weaknesses:
- Graves is a waist-bender, not a knee-bender, hindering his lower-body strength and ability to stay in his gap. He ends up losing gap control against one-on-one blocks from offensive linemen, who play with good leverage, and gets blown off the ball by combo blocks.

- Waist bending also causes issues with his balance, as he ends up with his nose over his toes often.

- Hands are often late and wide as a run defender, exposing his chest and leading to getting stuck on blocks often.

- Late to recognize and struggles to sink his hips against down blocks, leading to him getting washed inside.

- Use of hands as a pass-rusher is poor, showing issues with timing and accuracy, which makes it difficult for him to win consistently.

Name: Tyren Montgomery
Summary:
Tyren Montgomery is an athletic receiver with developmental traits to become a role player at the next level.

Montgomery is a former 0-star recruit in the 2019 class. who did not play football in high school. His career began as a basketball recruit for LSU before COVID shut down operations. Montgomery began learning the game of football and enrolling at Nichols State before transferring to JCU.

During his final two years at JCU, he amassed 176 receptions, 2,599 receiving yards, and 32 touchdowns.
Strengths:
- Three-level route runner from multiple receiver alignments.

- Runs routes with nuance and detailed movement patterns to attack defensive backs. Uses head fakes, rocket steps, and shoulder fakes to shake away from the defender.

- Basketball background translates to climbing the ladder and high-point passes. Attacks passes with strong hand-eye coordination.

- Athleticism and top-end speed to stack, separate, and win vertically.

- Occupying high traffic areas of the field; fights through contact to finish plays.
Weaknesses:
- Limited playing experience, started playing football collegiately in 2023.

- Faced lower level of competition.

- Needs more time on task/reps to fully develop into his skill set.

- Limiting indicators at the break point of his routes.

Name: Enrique Cruz Jr.
Summary:
Enrique Cruz Jr. is quick out of his stance with good length to initially cover up targets and plays with a red-hot motor that shows up in his effort to finish. However, his upright playing style and marginal redirect ability limit his upside. The concerns found within his game point toward a backup projection in the NFL, with his clearest path to sticking on a roster likely coming through developing positional flexibility at guard.

Cruz is a 6'5", 313-pound offensive line prospect, who entered the Syracuse program as a 4-star recruit in the 2021 class.

Cruz started 18 games at Syracuse (15 at left tackle, three at right tackle) over four seasons before transferring to Kansas in 2025 where he started 12 games at right tackle.
Strengths:
- Light-footed and quick out of his stance to reach his landmarks on time.

- Knows how to weaponize his length and width to cover up targets during his initial fit.

- Solid ability to re-leverage his hips against power to die slowly in his anchor.

- Excellent effort with a consistent sense of urgency who finishes to the ball.
Weaknesses:
- Upright play style with minimal power at the point of attack.

- Tardy redirect ability creates soft, easily accessible edges.

Name: Henry Lutovsky
Summary:
Henry Lutovsky brings very good size, length, and hand strength with solid power and linear explosiveness to create displacement. He plays with a fiery, physical demeanor but below-average coordination and body control lead to disjointed positions and inconsistent sustain skills. He brings enough traits and the right temperament to stick on a roster as a backup guard.

Lutovsky is a 6'6", 319-pound offensive guard prospect who entered the Nebraska program as a 3-star recruit in the 2021 class.

The former Cornhusker started 28 career games primarily at right (15) and left (13) guard, including 13 at left in 2025 as a team captain. He turned 23-years old in January.
Strengths:
- Very good size, length, and hand strength to create initial displacement.

- Generates force with linear explosiveness off the snap.

- Physical, high-motor demeanor that shows up through contact and to the whistle.

- Flashes functional athletic ability to steer and drive defenders.
Weaknesses:
- Below-average coordination leads to disjointed body positioning.

- Inconsistent sustain due to balance and body control issues post-contact.

Name: Desmond Reid
Summary:
Desmond Reid is an undersized change-of-pace running back prospect who is an impactful receiver out of the backfield.

The 5'8", 175-pound running back entered Western Carolina as a 3-star recruit in the 2022 class. He spent two productive years at Western Carolina before transferring to Pittsburgh.

In the NFL, Reid projects as a rotational running back in the passing game and a dynamic punt returner.
Strengths:
- Natural pass-catcher out of the backfield with ball-tracking ability on wheel/rail routes down the field.

- Quick and elusive mover in space to make defenders miss in a phone booth.

- Presses the line of scrimmage with patience and vision to locate open lanes.

- Runs hard and will lower his shoulder to finish a run and fall forward. Uses his leverage advantage to strike free-running linebackers in pass pro.

- Core special teams upside as a reliable punt returner.
Weaknesses:
- Undersized running back with a frame that will limit his role and usage in the NFL.

- Will not be viewed as a reliable short-yardage runner.

- Lacks play strength and physical mass to power through arm tackles. Early contact in the backfield can end the play almost simultaneously.

- Good speed but lacks the gear to pull away and be a home run hitter.

Name: James Brockermeyer
Summary:
James Brockermeyer is an undersized center prospect who wins with positional leverage, effort and an over-reliance on establishing first meaningful contact to circumvent his lack of sand. Without a true standout physical trait, he projects as a backup center in a zone-based run scheme in the NFL.

Brockermeyer is a 6'3", 295-pound center prospect who originally committed to Alabama with his twin brother as a 4-star recruit in the 2021 class. He appeared in 16 games over three years in Tuscaloosa before transferring to TCU with his brother for the 2024 season, making 12 starts at center. He then transferred again in 2025 to Miami, where he started 16 games, including the national championship against Indiana.

Brockermeyer started 28 games at center during his college career. He is the son of former NFL LT and 1995 first-round pick Blake Brockermeyer.
Strengths:
— Solid initial quickness and leverage skills.

— Gets into his fits quickly with proper positional leverage and tight, inside hand placement.

— Serviceable zone run-blocker who understands how/when to overtake on front- and back-side combinations.

— Strains and runs his feet to keep defenders tight and reduce space once latched.

— One of the most consistent performers during practice at the Senior Bowl.
Weaknesses:
— Below-average length, mass and sand puts a hard cap on his NFL ceiling.

— Struggles to root his feet and anchor against head-up nose tackles.

Name: Luke Altmyer
Summary:
Luke Atlmyer is an experienced and accomplished quarterback prospect with average physical tools but flashes good football IQ. Altmyer projects as a career backup quarterback due to his limited physical upside.

Initially, Altmyer began his career at Ole Miss. He enrolled as a former 4-star recruit in the 2021 class. He started 36 games in his career, with 35 coming at Illinois. He was named as an Honorable Mention All-Big Ten the last two seasons.

Dame Parson is the lead host of the Locked On NFL Draft podcast. He also serves as co-host of the Scouting Room NFL Podcast alongside Daniel Harms.
Strengths:
- Good processor post-snap; identifies the coverage before scanning to find an open receiver

- Comfortable in true passing situations; handles full-field reads and displays good football IQ

- Creates space for his targets versus zone coverages by manipulating defenders with his eyes

- Functional/above average athlete; scrambler who will convert first downs if unaccounted for
Weaknesses:
- Adequate arm strength to drive the football

- Inconsistent lower body mechanics; is not lined up to his target, leading to inaccuracies

- Difficulty throwing and fitting passes into windows versus tight-man coverage

- Decision-making declines under pressure; will put the ball in harm's way
